title: W7: Introduction to Distributed Computing
blurb: Medium-high priority. Earlier exams ask conceptual parallelism; recent exams add memory calculations.

knowledge_points.title: Data Parallelism and Model Parallelism
knowledge_points.recap: Distributed training is used either because training is too slow on one GPU or because the model does not fit on one GPU.

questions.q: 1. Explain what data parallelism and model parallelism are, and what challenges each addresses when training an LLM. (4 points)
questions.answer:
**Data parallelism:** The dataset is split into smaller batches that are processed in parallel across multiple devices. Each device has a copy of the model and processes a different subset of the data. **This is useful for speeding up training.**

**Model parallelism:** The model itself is split across multiple devices, with each device responsible for computing a portion of the model. Devices exchange intermediate results with each other. **This is useful for training very large models that do not fit into the memory of a single device.**
questions.extend: Memory: data parallel = split data, replicate model, speed. Model parallel = split model, solve memory/model-size limit.

questions.q: 2. Explain the difference between pipeline parallelism and tensor parallelism. In each case, describe how the model is split across GPUs. (2 points)
questions.answer:
**Pipeline parallelism:** the model is split between layers across GPUs, where each GPU holds a subset of consecutive layers, e.g. GPU 0 holds layers 1-3 and GPU 1 holds layers 4-6 **(1 point)**. During the forward pass, each GPU sends activations to the next GPU; during backward pass, activation gradients are passed back.

**Tensor parallelism:** the model is split within individual layers across GPUs, where each GPU holds a portion of the weight tensors of each layer, e.g. different attention heads in a transformer are assigned to different GPUs **(1 point)**. GPUs compute partial outputs for the same layer and combine them before moving to the next layer.
questions.extend: Memory: pipeline = split by layers; tensor = split inside a layer/tensor.

questions.q: Supplemental practice (not a direct exam question): Contrast data parallelism and tensor parallelism in terms of what is replicated and what is split across devices.
questions.answer: In data parallelism, each device usually has a full copy of the model and processes different data examples; gradients are synchronized across devices. In tensor parallelism, individual large tensor operations or weight matrices are split across devices, so each device holds and computes only part of a layer.
questions.extend: Memory: data parallel splits batch; tensor parallel splits layer tensors.

questions.q: Supplemental practice (not a direct exam question): Why can pipeline parallelism suffer from pipeline bubbles, and what does increasing microbatch count do?
questions.answer: Pipeline bubbles occur when some stages are idle while the pipeline is filling or draining. Splitting a batch into more microbatches keeps stages busy for a larger fraction of time, reducing idle bubbles. However, more microbatches can add scheduling overhead and may affect memory/latency tradeoffs.
questions.extend: Memory: pipeline parallelism improves utilization when enough microbatches fill the pipeline.

knowledge_points.title: Memory Accounting for Large Models
knowledge_points.recap: Memory questions require bytes per parameter and clear knowledge of what is replicated or sharded.

questions.q:
Consider training an $N$-parameter model with the Adam optimizer in mixed precision on a node where each GPU has 40 GB of memory. Ignore training-batch memory. Per-parameter storage requirements are: forward/backward weights: 2 bytes (BFloat16); gradients: 2 bytes (BFloat16); optimizer state (FP32): model weights for updates 4 bytes, Adam first moment $m$ 4 bytes, Adam second moment $v$ 4 bytes.

1. You want to train a 10B parameter model, $N=10^{10}$, using ZeRO-2. What is the minimum number of GPUs needed? (4 points)
questions.answer:
In **ZeRO-2, gradients and optimizer states are sharded across GPUs, while each GPU still stores the full forward/backward weights (1 point)**. For $G$ GPUs, memory per parameter per GPU is **(1 point)**

$$2+\frac{2}{G}+\frac{4+4+4}{G}=2+\frac{14}{G}\text{ bytes}.$$

The memory constraint is **(1 point)**

$$N\left(2+\frac{14}{G}\right)\le 40\times10^9.$$

Substitute $N=10^{10}$:

$$10^{10}\left(2+\frac{14}{G}\right)\le40\times10^9,$$

so

$$2+\frac{14}{G}\le4,\quad \frac{14}{G}\le2,\quad G\ge7.$$

The minimum number of GPUs is **$G=7$ (1 point)**.
questions.extend: Memory: ZeRO-2 shards gradients + optimizer state, but not forward/backward weights.

questions.q: Supplemental practice (not a direct exam question): Why does mixed precision reduce memory pressure?
questions.answer: **Mixed precision stores some tensors in lower precision**, such as BF16/FP16 forward weights and gradients, reducing bytes per parameter. Optimizer update states may still be kept in FP32 for numerical stability, so the memory saving depends on which tensors are low precision.
questions.extend: Memory: in the WS26 accounting table, BF16 weights and gradients are 2 bytes, FP32 optimizer states are 4 bytes each.

questions.q: Homework 9. Train an $N$-parameter model with Adam in mixed precision on 4 GPUs, each with 80 GB. Per parameter: weights 2 bytes, gradients 2 bytes, optimizer state has update weights, Adam $m$, Adam $v$, each 4 bytes. Compute maximum $N$ without ZeRO, with ZeRO-1, ZeRO-2, and ZeRO-3.
questions.answer:
**No ZeRO:** $2+2+4+4+4=16$ bytes/parameter, so $N\le80\times10^9/16=\mathbf{5.0\times10^9}$.

**ZeRO-1:** shard optimizer state only: $2+2+12/4=7$ bytes/parameter, so **$N\approx1.14\times10^{10}$**.

**ZeRO-2:** shard gradients and optimizer state: $2+2/4+12/4=5.5$ bytes/parameter, so **$N\approx1.45\times10^{10}$**.

**ZeRO-3:** shard weights, gradients, and optimizer state: $2/4+2/4+12/4=4$ bytes/parameter, so **$N=2.0\times10^{10}$**.
questions.extend: Memory: ZeRO-1 shards optimizer, ZeRO-2 also gradients, ZeRO-3 also parameters.

questions.q: Supplemental practice (not a direct exam question): In mixed-precision Adam training, why can optimizer states consume more memory than the model parameters themselves?
questions.answer: Adam keeps extra state such as first and second moment estimates, often in higher precision. Training may also keep master weights and gradients. Therefore each parameter can require several stored values, so optimizer state memory can exceed the memory of the model weights alone.
questions.extend: Memory: Adam is expensive because it stores moments, not just weights.

questions.q: Supplemental practice (not a direct exam question): What is the main memory-saving idea of ZeRO compared with ordinary data parallel training?
questions.answer: Ordinary data parallel training replicates parameters, gradients, and optimizer states on every device. ZeRO partitions some of these states across devices, so each device stores only a shard. The stage determines whether optimizer states, gradients, and/or parameters are sharded.
questions.extend: Memory: ZeRO saves memory by sharding training states across data-parallel workers.

knowledge_points.title: MoE Routing, Load Balance, and Memory
knowledge_points.recap: Tutorial MoE focuses on auxiliary load-balancing losses, device-level expert balance, bias-based routing correction, and memory/active-parameter accounting for sparse MoE layers.

questions.q:
MoE auxiliary load-balancing loss is

$$L_{balance}=N\sum_i f_iP_i.$$

For $N=4$, expert token fractions are $0.70,0.20,0.10,0$ and average router probabilities are $0.6,0.25,0.10,0.05$. Compute the loss, the perfectly balanced value, and identify which expert receives the largest balance-loss update.
questions.answer:
The loss is

$$4(0.70\cdot0.60+0.20\cdot0.25+0.10\cdot0.10+0\cdot0.05)=\mathbf{1.92}.$$

Perfect balance has $f_i=P_i=1/4$, so **$L_{balance}=1$**.

Ignoring the non-differentiable top-$K$ path through $f_i$, $\partial L/\partial P_i=Nf_i$. The largest update is therefore for **expert 1**, because $f_1=0.70$ is largest.
questions.extend: Memory: $L_{balance}=1$ under perfect balance; overloaded experts get larger updates.

questions.q: MoE experts are partitioned across $D$ devices. Let $E_d$ be the experts on device $d$. Define a device-level load-balancing loss and compare it to DeepSeek-V2.
questions.answer:
Aggregate per device:

$$f_d^*=\sum_{i\in E_d}f_i,\qquad P_d^*=\sum_{i\in E_d}P_i.$$

A direct loss is

$$L_{device}=D\sum_d f_d^*P_d^*,$$

which equals **1** under perfect device balance.

DeepSeek-V2 instead uses average per-expert utilization,

$$f_d'=\frac{1}{|E_d|}\sum_{i\in E_d}f_i,$$

with $P_d'$ summed over device experts. The distinction is **total device load versus average expert load on the device**.
questions.extend: Memory: DeepSeek-V2 normalizes device load by number of experts.

questions.q: DeepSeek-V3 auxiliary-loss-free balancing adds online expert biases. With top-1 routing, $\gamma=0.01$, initial $b_i=0$, and scores $s_1=0.9,s_2=0.85,s_3=0.40,s_4=0.30$, expert 1 is overloaded and expert 4 underloaded for 10 steps. Which expert is selected after 10 updates?
questions.answer:
At step 0, **expert 1** is selected. After 10 updates,

$$b_1=-0.10,\quad b_4=+0.10,\quad b_2=b_3=0.$$

Biased scores are

$$0.80,\quad0.85,\quad0.40,\quad0.40.$$

The selected expert is **expert 2**. The bias changes expert selection but **does not change the gate weights used in the forward pass**.
questions.extend: Memory: routing bias is an online steering signal, not a gradient-learned gate weight.

questions.q: GLM-4.5 MoE layer: $d_{model}=5120$, 160 routed experts plus 1 shared expert, top-$K=8$, fine-grained ratio $1/4$. Each expert has two matrices and parameter count $2d_{model}d_{ff}^{expert}$. With bf16, compute one-layer memory and active parameter fraction.
questions.answer:
The expert hidden size is

$$d_{ff}^{expert}=\frac14\cdot4d_{model}=5120.$$

Each expert has $2\cdot5120\cdot5120\approx52.4$M parameters. With 161 experts, total parameters are **about 8.4B**, requiring **about 16.9 GB** in bf16.

Only $K+1=9$ experts are active per token, so the active fraction is **$9/161\approx5.6\%$**.
questions.extend: Memory: MoE stores all experts but activates only top-$K$ plus shared experts.

questions.q: MoE homework. A dense transformer has 7B parameters. Replacing every feedforward layer by an MoE layer with 8 experts gives a 49B-parameter model. With top-$K=2$, compute active parameters.
questions.answer:
Let $x$ be non-feedforward parameters and $y$ feedforward parameters. Then

$$x+y=7,\qquad x+8y=49.$$

Solving gives **$x=1$**, **$y=6$**. With top-$K=2$, active parameters are

$$x+2y=1+12=\mathbf{13B}.$$
questions.extend: Memory: active MoE parameters use top-$K$ expert copies, not all experts.

questions.q: MoE homework. Compare a dense 49B model with the previous 49B MoE model that has 13B active parameters. Assume compute scales linearly with active parameters. How do compute and memory compare?
questions.answer:
Both models require **roughly the same parameter memory**, because all 49B MoE parameters must be loaded.

The MoE model uses only 13B active parameters per forward pass, so it is faster by approximately

$$\frac{49}{13}\approx\mathbf{3.8}.$$

MoE saves compute but not total parameter storage.
questions.extend: Memory: MoE decouples active compute from loaded parameters.

questions.q: Supplemental practice (not a direct exam question): Why does an MoE model distinguish total parameters from active parameters per token?
questions.answer: An MoE model may contain many experts, so the total parameter count can be very large. But each token is routed to only a small number of experts, so the active parameters and compute per token are much smaller than using all experts. This is the main sparsity advantage of MoE.
questions.extend: Memory: total parameters affect storage; active parameters affect per-token compute.

questions.q: Supplemental practice (not a direct exam question): Why is load balancing important in top-k expert routing?
questions.answer: If many tokens route to the same expert, that expert becomes a compute and communication bottleneck while other experts are underused. Load balancing encourages tokens to spread across experts, improving device utilization and preventing expert collapse.
questions.extend: Memory: routing quality includes both specialization and balanced utilization.
